original_abstract: Mitigating toxic NO emissions remains a primary challenge in environmental catalysis, particularly for vehicle aftertreatment systems. In this study, we utilize an atom-trapping approach to synthesize a novel class of ceria-supported catalysts featuring atomically dispersed Ru(II) ions. Characterization by HAADF-STEM, EXAFS, and DFT calculations confirms the presence of isolated $Ru_1O_5$ sites localized on the (100) facets of ceria, an environment distinct from the square-planar coordination typically observed for Pt and Pd single atoms. These $Ru_1/CeO_2$ materials exhibit exceptional activity for NO oxidation, significantly outperforming their Pt and Pd counterparts and maintaining stability in the presence of moisture and under hydrothermal aging up to 750 °C. Furthermore, the catalysts demonstrate unprecedented NOx storage capacity, achieving full NO consumption at 100 °C with low Ru loadings (0.05–0.5 wt%). Mechanistic investigations using in situ DRIFTS, mass spectrometry, and modulation-excitation spectroscopy reveal that Ru(II) ions act as unique "shuttles" that facilitate efficient NOx spill-over onto the ceria support as nitrites and nitrates, while also forming stable Ru-nitrosyl complexes. Additionally, $Ru_1/CeO_2$ shows high efficiency in the reduction of NO by CO at low temperatures. We identified the direct participation of oxygen vacancies at the Ru-Ce interface as a critical factor in the reaction mechanism. These findings highlight the potential of dilute single-atom Ru catalysts as cost-effective, high-performance alternatives for the next generation of emission control technologies.
humanized_abstract: A formidable challenge in environmental catalysis is reduction of toxic nitrogen oxides (NOx) in engine after-treatment systems. Here, we show that atom trapping of Ru results in highly active and durable catalysts that consist of atomically dispersed Ru(II) species supported on ceria. These are anchored specifically to the (100) facets of ceria nanocrystals in a $Ru_1O_5$ configuration, which is square pyramidal, a configuration different from the typically seen for singly dispersed Pt and Pd species (trigonal bipyramid and square planar respectively). Catalysts of this $Ru_1/CeO_2$ composition exhibit a record-high catalytic activity for NO oxidation, exceeding all previously reported platinum and palladium-based single-atom NO oxidation catalysts, while being stable under various conditions, such as under both wet and hydrothermally-aged conditions up to 750 °C. The catalytic performance is unprecedented, as these single-atom Ru catalysts can store up to 4-fold more NOx than their platinum-based counter parts while using only 0.05-0.5 wt% Ru, and achieve the 100 °C light-off temperature for the whole NOx over the full concentration range. Using a multifaceted approach of in situ DRIFTS, mass spectrometry, and modulation-excitation (MOD) spectroscopy, we show that these Ru species serve as “shuttles” to oxidize nitric oxide to nitrites and nitrates and simultaneously act as nitrosyl precursors in this system. The $Ru_1/CeO_2$ catalyst also shows an outstanding CO-low-temperature activity for the selective reduction of NO by CO. Finally, the involvement of O V over the Ru-Ce interface in the catalytic cycle is evidenced by modulated excitation experiments and attributed to the crucial role of the heterointerface between the metallic and oxide components in enabling this heterogeneous redox reaction. These findings pave the way towards designing efficient dilute single-atom catalysts in a cost-effective manner for next generation catalysts required for reducing environmental harmful emissions.
